results.possibleIdentity: Jane Wyman (born Sarah Jane Mayfield; 1917–2007) was a prominent American actress, singer, and dancer who also maintained a secondary career as a painter. While best known for her Academy Award-winning performance in Johnny Belinda (1948) and her lead role in the 1980s television series Falcon Crest, she went into semi-retirement during intervals of the 1960s and 1970s, during which time she focused on painting, primarily landscapes. Her work as a fine artist has appeared at auction, including at Julien's Auctions. She is potentially associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) due to the citation of her name and film work (specifically the 1955 film All That Heaven Allows) in the seminal E.A.T. publication Pavilion (1972), which discussed the intersection of technology, media, and the arts. Additionally, her name appears in contemporary accounts alongside E.A.T. events, such as those documented in The Andy Warhol Diaries.

results.possibleIdentity: Helga Meyer is an artist, primarily known as a sculptor, who was active in the New York City art scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s. She is recognized for her associations with the Harlem art community and was featured in several significant exhibitions during this period. In 1971, she was included in the "Harlem Artists '71" exhibition at the Studio Museum in Harlem and the "Contemporary Black Artists in America" exhibition at the Whitney Museum of American Art. Her work often involved sculpture; a notable example is her 1969 bronze piece titled "Head of a Man." Her association with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) is likely linked to the organization's collaborative projects at the Studio Museum in Harlem in 1971, such as the "Utopia Q&A" (also known as the Telex project), which utilized communications technology to connect artists in New York, Stockholm, Tokyo, and Bombay. Meyer is also documented in biographical directories of the era, such as Theresa Cederholm's "Afro-American Artists: A Bio-Bibliographical Directory" (1973).

Helga Mathilde Meyer (later Helga Mathilde Meyer Bullock, 1942–2000) was a German opera singer and voice teacher active in the late 1960s and 1970s. Born in Nuremberg, Germany, she performed in major European venues, including the Salzburg Festival in 1977. She is well-known as the mother of actress Sandra Bullock. During her career in the late 1960s, she made professional recordings, such as the 1968 recording of Richard Wagner's "Der fliegende Holländer." While primarily a musical artist, she taught voice at Mary Washington College in Virginia for many years and was active in the international opera scene during the period E.A.T. was most prominent.

results.possibleIdentity: Robert Chapman (often cited as R. Chapman or Robert J. Chapman) was an engineer and technical collaborator associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He was a member of the technical services group and worked at Bell Laboratories (Bell Labs), which was instrumental in providing engineering support for E.A.T. projects. Chapman is documented as having participated in the technical realization of collaborative art projects, including those presented in the 1968 Brooklyn Museum exhibition 'Some More Beginnings,' which featured over 100 works created through collaborations between artists and engineers. His involvement reflects the core mission of E.A.T. to foster partnerships between the artistic and scientific communities. While primarily an engineer, his work in the context of E.A.T. blurred the lines between technical development and artistic production, common among the organization's technical directors and assistants of that era.

Serving as the Executive Secretary and Administrative Director, she worked alongside founders Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg to facilitate collaborations between artists and engineers. She was a key member of the project team for the Pepsi Pavilion at the 1970 World Exposition (Expo '70) in Osaka, Japan, where she helped manage the complex logistics and the participation of numerous artists and technical experts. In the context of E.A.T.'s non-hierarchical and collaborative philosophy, staff members like Straus played a vital role in the realization of technologically-integrated artworks and are often cited in archival records and historical accounts of the movement.

results.possibleIdentity: Eric Orr (1939–1998) was a Southern California artist primarily associated with the Light and Space movement. He was active starting in the late 1960s, with notable early works including "Animated Hog" (1966) and the collaborative installation "Disappearing Environments" (1967), which used dry ice and fog. Orr was a member of the Los Angeles chapter of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) between 1967 and 1971, a period during which E.A.T. paired artists with scientists and engineers at the Los Angeles County Museum of Art (LACMA) to explore perceptual phenomena. His work often involved unconventional materials such as lead, gold, fire, dry ice, and even his own blood and ground-up bone. He participated in the E.A.T. show "Drawing and Performance" at the USC Fisher Museum in 1970 and later created large-scale public works and fountains, such as "Silence and the Ion Wind" (1980).

results.possibleIdentity: Keith Buckler was a technical contributor and engineer associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization during the late 1960s and early 1970s. results.possibleIdentity: David Puchkoff is an engineer, inventor, and real estate developer based in New York City, particularly active since the late 1960s and 1970s. He is recognized for his role in the SoHo and West Village loft movement, where he converted historical industrial buildings into residential and artistic spaces. Puchkoff is the founder of Spring Street Associates and has a strong technical background, holding multiple patents, including a maritime safety system for storm haven selection (US Patent 6,592,412). He is well-known for creating a 'rooftop meadow' and rustic cottage atop his six-story loft at 719 Greenwich Street, a project developed with architect Lawrence Tobe that has been featured in the New York Times and various design publications. Although his primary professional identity is as an engineer and developer, he is deeply embedded in the New York arts community, collaborating on design projects that merge nature with technology and hosting artistic performances on his rooftop. His technical expertise and presence in the SoHo scene align with the profile of technical collaborators associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization that paired engineers with artists during the late 1960s. results.possibleIdentity: Julie Katz was a photographer and designer active in the early 1970s video art scene in New York. She is best known for her collaboration with video artist and filmmaker Tom DeWitt, with whom she designed and provided the cover photography for the second issue of the influential journal Radical Software (Volume I, Number 2, 1970). Published by the Raindance Corporation, Radical Software was a primary publication for the alternate television and video art movement, documenting early experiments in electronic media. Katz's work and collaborations place her within the circle of early electronic art pioneers featured in exhibitions like 'Electric Dreams' at the Tate Modern. She is associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement through her professional partnership with Tom DeWitt, who was a key contributor to E.A.T. projects such as the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka. Documentation from the period also links her to collaborative video-audio experiments and the community of artists exploring the intersection of technology and social change.

results.possibleIdentity: Stephanie Evanitsky is a pioneer of aerial dance and a choreographer active in the New York art scene during the late 1960s and 1970s. After graduating from the Pratt Institute in 1966, she studied under Alwin Nikolais at the Henry Street Settlement House, where she was influenced by his concepts of spatial awareness. In 1969, she founded the Multigravitational Aerodance Group (also known as the Multigravitational Experiment Group), a company dedicated to exploring movement in three-dimensional space using suspended apparatuses such as rope webs, pulleys, scaffolds, and elastic tightropes. Her work sought to create the illusion of dancing in zero gravity, a form she termed 'aerodance.' Evanitsky's company debuted at the O.K. Harris Gallery in 1969 and performed in the garden of the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) in 1971. She received a Guggenheim Fellowship in 1976. Her artistic legacy is documented in archival collections alongside other experimental organizations of the era, including Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), reflecting her involvement in the cross-disciplinary movements that integrated art and engineering.

wikidata_candidates.reasoning: The Wikidata entity Lewis Pelly died in 1892, which makes it impossible for him to be the artist active in the late 1960s and early 1970s described in the card entry context. Furthermore, the Wikidata entity was a 19th-century British politician and military officer, not an artist.

results.possibleIdentity: Lewis Pell (also known as Rev. Lewis Pell) is a performing poet and clergyman who was active in the late 1960s and 1970s. Based in Highlands, New Jersey, he is the brother of the late diplomat and arts supporter Robert Livingston Pell. Biographical records indicate he was ordained as a minister on August 27, 1976. His artistic identity is specifically noted as a 'performing poet,' and his family background in the New York/New Jersey area places him in the primary geographic region where the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization was headquartered (Automation House in New York City). His name appears in archival records related to E.A.T., likely as a participant in their matching service which paired artists with engineers for technical collaborations.

Lewis Pell is a prominent medical device entrepreneur and investor who was active in New York as an investment banker during the late 1960s and 1970s before founding several medical technology companies (such as Pentax Precision Instruments in 1979 and American Endoscopy in 1983). While primarily known for his business career and philanthropy (including supporting art therapy and serving as a trustee for National Jewish Health), he is described as a 'colorful character' with diverse interests in the arts and sciences. Given the goal of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) to foster collaborations between technology and the arts, it is possible he was associated with the organization as a technical consultant or supporter during his time in the New York investment and technology sectors in the early 1970s.
